subject: Physics
q: An ideal fluid of density 800 kg m-3, flows smoothly through a bent pipe (as shown in figure) that tapers in cross-sectional area from a to a2. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of pipe is 4100 Pa. At wider section, the velocity of fluid is x6 m s-1 for x=_____ . (Given g=10 m s-2)
question: An ideal fluid of density 800 kg m-3, flows smoothly through a bent pipe (as shown in figure) that tapers in cross-sectional area from a to a2. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of pipe is 4100 Pa. At wider section, the velocity of fluid is x6 m s-1 for x=_____ . (Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
From equation of continuity,

av1=a2v2⇒v2=2v1

From Bernoulli's theorem,

P1+12ρv12+ρgh1=P2+12ρv22+ρgh2⇒P1-P2=ρgh2-h1+12v22-v12⇒4100=80010-1+123v12⇒4100800+10=32v12⇒v1=1214×3=3636 m s-1

Therefore, x=363.

q: When two soap bubbles of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>></mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo></math> coalesce, the radius of curvature of common surface is:

solution:
Excess pressure at common surface is given by

Pex=4T1a-1b=4Tr

∴1r=1a-1 b

r=abb-a

subject: Physics
q:
In the experiment for measurement of viscosity ' $\eta$ ' of given liquid with a ball having radius $R$, consider following statements.
A. Graph between terminal velocity V and R will be a parabola.
B. The terminal velocities of different diameter balls are constant for a given liquid.
C. Measurement of terminal velocity is dependent on the temperature.
D. This experiment can be utilized to assess the density of a given liquid.
E. If balls are dropped with some initial speed, the value of $\eta$ will change.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In the experiment for measurement of viscosity ' $\eta$ ' of given liquid with a ball having radius $R$, consider following statements.
A. Graph between terminal velocity V and R will be a parabola.
B. The terminal velocities of different diameter balls are constant for a given liquid.
C. Measurement of terminal velocity is dependent on the temperature.
D. This experiment can be utilized to assess the density of a given liquid.
E. If balls are dropped with some initial speed, the value of $\eta$ will change.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The terminal velocity of ball of radius $R$ inside a liquid of viscosity $\eta$ can be written as $V_T=\frac{2 R^2 g}{n}(\sigma-\rho)$, where $\sigma$ is the density of ball and $\delta$ is the density of the liquid.
Hence,
$A$ is correct since $V_T \propto R^2$ gives a parabola on a graph
$C$ is correct since $V_T \alpha \frac{1}{\eta}$ and $\eta$ varies with temperature
$D$ is correct since $V_T \alpha(\sigma-\rho)$ i.e., varies with density of liquid.

solution:
Using equation of continuity
A1V1=A2V2
V1V2=A2A1=4.86.42=916

subject: Physics
q: The surface tension of a soap bubble is $0.03$ N/m. The work done in increasing the diameter of bubble from $2$ cm to $6$ cm is $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take $\pi = 3.14$)
question: The surface tension of a soap bubble is $0.03$ N/m. The work done in increasing the diameter of bubble from $2$ cm to $6$ cm is $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
Initial radius $r_1 = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1$ cm $= 10^{-2}$ m

Final radius $r_2 = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$ cm $= 3 \times 10^{-2}$ m

Surface tension $T = 0.03$ N/m

A soap bubble has two free surfaces. The change in total surface area is given by:

$\Delta A = 2 \times 4\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$

$\Delta A = 8\pi ((3 \times 10^{-2})^2 - (10^{-2})^2)$

$\Delta A = 8\pi (9 \times 10^{-4} - 10^{-4}) = 64\pi \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$

Work done $W = T \Delta A$

$W = 0.03 \times 64\pi \times 10^{-4}$

$W = 1.92\pi \times 10^{-4}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J, we get $\alpha = 1.92$.

Answer: $1.92$

subject: Physics
q: The terminal velocity of a small sphere of radius $a$ in a viscous liquid is proportional to
question: The terminal velocity of a small sphere of radius $a$ in a viscous liquid is proportional to
solution:
Terminal velocity in a viscous medium is given by:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& V_T=\frac{2 a^2(\rho-\sigma) g}{9 \eta} \\
& \therefore V_T \propto a^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum pressure the smaller piston would have to bear is [Assume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A hydraulic automobile lift is designed to lift vehicles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The area of cross section of the cylinder carrying load is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The maximum pressure the smaller piston would have to bear is [Assume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
The given data is

m=5000 kgg=10 m s-2A=250×10-4 m2

Using the formula of pressure,

P=FA=mgA
=5000×10250×10-4

=2×106 N m-2=2×106 Pa

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
question: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
solution:
$$ \text { Let the rate of falling water level be }-\frac{d h}{d t} $$
Initially at $t=0 ; h=h$ $$ t=t ; h=0 $$ Then, $A\left(-\frac{d h}{d t}\right)=\pi a^2 \cdot v$ $$ d t=-\frac{A}{\pi a^2 \sqrt{2 g h}} d h $$ $[\because$ velocity of efflux of liquid $v=\sqrt{2 g h}]$ Integrating both sides $$ \int_0^t d t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \int_h^0 h^{-1 / 2} d h $$ $$ [t]_0^t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \cdot\left[\frac{h^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right]_h^0 $$ $$ t=\frac{\sqrt{2} A}{\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{g}} $$

subject: Physics
q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> contain water upto heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The vessels are interconnected so that the water levels in them become equal. The work done by the force of gravity during the process, is [Take density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> contain water upto heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The vessels are interconnected so that the water levels in them become equal. The work done by the force of gravity during the process, is [Take density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Initially, the centre of mass of the right column will be at height of 75 cm and the centre of mass of the left column will be at 50 cm. After opening of the valve both columns will have height of 125 cm and their centre of mass will be at height 1252 cm. Therefore,

W=-∆U=-m1+m2gh1+h24-m1gh12-m2gh22

Now, m1=ρAh1 and m2=ρAh2

Putting ρ=1000 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2, h1=100 cm, h2=150 cm and A=16×10-4 m2, we get,

W=1 J

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Pressure of a fluid is exerted only on a solid surface in contact as the fluid-pressure does not exist everywhere in a still fluid.
Statement II: Excess potential energy of the molecules on the surface of a liquid, when compared to interior, results in surface tension.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Pressure of a fluid is exerted only on a solid surface in contact as the fluid-pressure does not exist everywhere in a still fluid.
Statement II: Excess potential energy of the molecules on the surface of a liquid, when compared to interior, results in surface tension.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Statement I is false: Fluid pressure exists everywhere in a still fluid, not only on solid surfaces. According to Pascal's principle, pressure at any point in a static fluid acts equally in all directions and throughout the fluid volume.

Statement II is true: Surface tension results from excess potential energy of molecules at the liquid surface. Surface molecules experience net inward attractive forces from interior molecules, creating an imbalance that increases their potential energy and manifests as surface tension acting along the surface.

subject: Physics
q:
Mercury is filled in a tube of radius $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ up to a height of $30 \mathrm{~cm}$. The force exerted by mercury on the bottom of the tube is _____ N.
(Given, atmospheric pressure $=10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, density of mercury $=1.36 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-}$ $\left.{ }^3, \mathrm{~g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}, \pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
question:
Mercury is filled in a tube of radius $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ up to a height of $30 \mathrm{~cm}$. The force exerted by mercury on the bottom of the tube is _____ N.
(Given, atmospheric pressure $=10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, density of mercury $=1.36 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-}$ $\left.{ }^3, \mathrm{~g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}, \pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{P}_0 \mathrm{~A}+\rho_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{ghA} \\ & =10^5 \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \\ & +1.36 \times 10^4 \times 10 \times\left(30 \times 10^{-2}\right)\left(\frac{22}{7} \times\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2\right) \\ & \mathrm{F}=51.29+125.71=177 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A plane is in level flight at constant speed and each of its two wings has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of the air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the lower wing surface and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>252</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the upper wing surface, the mass of the plane is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Take air density to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A plane is in level flight at constant speed and each of its two wings has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of the air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the lower wing surface and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>252</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the upper wing surface, the mass of the plane is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Take air density to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Total wing span area, A=2×40=80 m2

Using Bernoulli's equation, we get uplift force as

AP2-P1=12ρV12-V22A

⇒mg=12×1702-502×80

⇒mg=2400×40

⇒m=9600 kg

f≥ρav2

subject: Physics
q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_1=(\rho \mathrm{A} \times 10) \mathrm{g} \times 5+(\rho \mathrm{A} 6) \mathrm{g} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\rho \mathrm{Ag}(50+18) \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=68 \rho \mathrm{Ag}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & U_f=(\rho A \times 16) g \times 4 \\ & =(\rho A g) \times 64\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta U=4 \times \rho A g \\ & =4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a hydraulic lift, the surface area of the input piston is \(6 \mathrm{~cm}^2\) and that of the output piston is \(1500 \mathrm{~cm}^2\). If 100 N force is applied to the input piston to raise the output piston by 20 cm, then the work done is _______ kJ.
question: In a hydraulic lift, the surface area of the input piston is \(6 \mathrm{~cm}^2\) and that of the output piston is \(1500 \mathrm{~cm}^2\). If 100 N force is applied to the input piston to raise the output piston by 20 cm, then the work done is _______ kJ.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{\mathrm{~A}_2}, \frac{100}{6}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{1500}, \mathrm{~F}=\frac{50}{3} \times 1500 \\ & \mathrm{~F}=50 \times 500=25 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \omega=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=25 \times 10^3 \times \frac{20}{100} \\ & =5 \times 10^3=5 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Figure below shows a liquid being pushed out of the tube by a piston having area of cross section 2.0 cm2. The area of cross section at the outlet is 10 mm2. If the piston is pushed at a speed of 4 cm s-1, the speed of outgoing fluid is _____ cm s-1
question: Figure below shows a liquid being pushed out of the tube by a piston having area of cross section 2.0 cm2. The area of cross section at the outlet is 10 mm2. If the piston is pushed at a speed of 4 cm s-1, the speed of outgoing fluid is _____ cm s-1
solution:
The given data is 

A1=2×10-4 m2A2=10×10-6 m2v1=4 cm s-1

Using equation of continuity which is a conservation of mass principle,

A1v1=A2v2

⇒v2=A1v1A2=2×10-4×410×10-6

= 80 cm s-1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Pressure in a reservoir of water is same at all points at the same level of water.

Statement II: The pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted in all directions equally.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Pressure in a reservoir of water is same at all points at the same level of water.

Statement II: The pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted in all directions equally.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
solution:
Pressure in a static fluid will be same at each point on same horizontal level.

P=P0+ρgh

where P0 is atmospheric pressure.

Pascal's law states that pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted equally in all directions. Hence, both the statements are true and this is the right option.

question: Correct Bernoulli's equation is (symbols have their usual meaning) :

subject: Physics
q: Eight equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the drops coalesce, the new velocity is:-
question: Eight equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the drops coalesce, the new velocity is:-
solution:
The terminal velocity can be given by 

vT=2(ρ-σ)r2g9η.

Clearly, vT∝r2

Let the radius of the small drops be r1 and the radius of the big drop be r2.

So,

 84π3r13=4π3r23⇒r1=r22

Hence, taking the ratio
vT1vT2=r1r22
⇒10vT2=14
⇒vT2=40 cm s-1

subject: Physics
q: A drop of liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> is floating half immersed in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> and surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The radius of drop in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : (Take : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A drop of liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> is floating half immersed in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> and surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The radius of drop in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : (Take : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Balancing the forces on drop 

Buoyant force + surface tension =mg

σV2g+2πRT=ρVg

2πRT=2ρ-σ2·43πR3 g;      As V=43πR3

⇒R3=3T2ρ-σg⇒R=3×7.5×10-2 N-m-12ρ-σ×10

R=3202ρ-σ m=152ρ-σ cm

subject: Physics
q: What will be the nature of flow of water from a circular tap, when its flow rate increased from 0.18 L min-1 to 0.48 L min-1? The radius of the tap and viscosity of water are 0.5 cm and 10-3 Pa s, respectively. (Density of water : 103 kg m-3)
question: What will be the nature of flow of water from a circular tap, when its flow rate increased from 0.18 L min-1 to 0.48 L min-1? The radius of the tap and viscosity of water are 0.5 cm and 10-3 Pa s, respectively. (Density of water : 103 kg m-3)
solution:
The nature of flow is determined by Reynolds
Number. 

Re=ρvDη

ρ→ density of fluid ;  η→ coefficient of velocityv→ velocity of flow D→ Diameterofpipe 

From NCERT 

If Re<1000 → flow is steady

1000<Rp<2000→ flow becomes unsteady

Re>2000→ flow is turbulent 

Reinitial =103×0.18×10-3π×0.5×10-22×60×1×10-210-3

=382.16

Re final=103×0.48×10-3π×0.5×10-22×60×1×10-210-3

=1019.09

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> rises from the bottom of a lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep to the surface at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The atmospheric pressure is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo></math>There is no difference of the temperature of water at the depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be
question: An air bubble of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> rises from the bottom of a lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep to the surface at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The atmospheric pressure is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo></math>There is no difference of the temperature of water at the depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be
solution:
The pressure at the bottom of the lake can be calculated as follows:

Pbottom= P0 + ρgh=105+1000 × 10 × 40 Pa=5×105 Pa 

As the temperature of water remains constant, it can be written that

PbottomVbottom=PtopVtop   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface.

5×105×1=1×105×Vtop⇒Vtop=5×105×11×105 cm3= 5 cm3

subject: Physics
q:
A small metallic sphere of diameter 2 mm and density $10.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ is dropped in glycerine having viscosity 10 Poise and density $1.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ respectively. The terminal velocity attained by the sphere is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$. 
($\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question:
A small metallic sphere of diameter 2 mm and density $10.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ is dropped in glycerine having viscosity 10 Poise and density $1.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ respectively. The terminal velocity attained by the sphere is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$. 
($\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Using Stokes' Law for terminal velocity:
$v_t = \frac{2r^2g(\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9\eta}$.
Given: r = 0.1 cm, g = 1000 cm/s², $\rho_s = 10.5$ g/cm³, $\rho_f = 1.5$ g/cm³, η = 10 Poise = 10 g/(cm⋅s).
Substituting:
$v_t = \frac{2 \times (0.1)^2 \times 1000 \times (10.5 - 1.5)}{9 \times 10}$
$= \frac{2 \times 0.01 \times 1000 \times 9}{90}$
$= \frac{180}{90} = 2.0$ cm/s.

subject: Physics
q: A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm. If $(15000-x) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm, then the value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\pi=22 / 7$)
question: A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm. If $(15000-x) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm, then the value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\pi=22 / 7$)
solution:
Soap bubble has two surfaces. Work done: $W = \Delta A \times \sigma$
Initial radius: $r_i = 3.5$ cm = 0.035 m, Final radius: $r_f = 7$ cm = 0.07 m
Change in surface area (both surfaces): $\Delta A = 8\pi(r_f^2 - r_i^2) = 8\pi(0.07^2 - 0.035^2)$
$\Delta A = 8\pi(0.0049 - 0.001225) = 8\pi \times 0.003675 = 0.0294\pi$ m²
$W = 0.0294\pi \times 0.04 = 0.001176\pi$ J = $0.001176 \times \frac{22}{7} = 0.003696$ J = 3696 μJ
Therefore: $x = 15000 - 3696 = 11304$

subject: Physics
q: A small rigid spherical ball of mass M is dropped in a long vertical tube containing glycerine. The velocity of the ball becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is half of the density of the ball, then the viscous force acting on the ball will be (consider g as acceleration due to gravity)
question: A small rigid spherical ball of mass M is dropped in a long vertical tube containing glycerine. The velocity of the ball becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is half of the density of the ball, then the viscous force acting on the ball will be (consider g as acceleration due to gravity)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{f}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{mg}-\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{2}-\mathrm{f}=0 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius r in the vertical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, Whose densities are <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> fill half the circle. The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is:
question: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius r in the vertical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, Whose densities are <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> fill half the circle. The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is:
solution:
Pressure at the point A is the same on both sides of the tube, 
ρ 1 gr(cosθ−sinθ)= ρ 2 gr(sinθ+cosθ)
⇒ρ1ρ2=sinθ+cosθcosθ-sinθ=tanθ+11-tanθ
⇒ρ1-ρ1tanθ=ρ2+ρ2tanθ
⇒ρ1+ρ2tanθ=ρ1-ρ2

θ=tan-1⁡ρ1-ρ2ρ1+ρ2

subject: Physics
q: There is an air bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> in a liquid of surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>075</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> below the free surface. The amount by which the pressure inside the bubble is greater than the atmospheric pressure is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
question: There is an air bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> in a liquid of surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>075</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> below the free surface. The amount by which the pressure inside the bubble is greater than the atmospheric pressure is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
solution:
The pressure inside the bubble is given by 

ΔP=P-P0=ρgh+2Tr

The data given is 

r=10-3 mT=0.075 N m-1ρ=1000 kg m-3h=10 cm

Therefore, the pressure in the bubble is 

ΔP=1000×10×10×10-2+2×0.07510-3=1000+150=1150 Pa

subject: Physics
q: Assume that a drop of a liquid evaporates by a decrease in its surface energy so that its temperature remains unchanged. The minimum radius of the drop for this to be possible is. (The surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, the density of the liquid is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is its latent heat of vaporisation.)
question: Assume that a drop of a liquid evaporates by a decrease in its surface energy so that its temperature remains unchanged. The minimum radius of the drop for this to be possible is. (The surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, the density of the liquid is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is its latent heat of vaporisation.)
solution:
Let the radius of the drop at time t=t be r and at an instant t=t+dt be r-dr

As surface area is given by, A=4πr2,

∴ decrease in surface area is,

dA=4π2rdr

∴ Decrease in surface energy during time dt is,

dU=TdA=T·8πrdr     ...1

Decrease in volume during time dt is,

dV=4πr2dr

∴ Decrease in mass during time dt is,

dm=ρdV=4πρr2dr

∴ Heat required in vaporisation is,

dQ=Ldm=4πρr2drL    ...2

Apply conservation of energy,

decrease in surface energy=heat required in vaporisation.

⇒ dU=dQ

⇒ 8Tπrdr=4πρr2drL

⇒2T=ρrL

⇒r=2TρL

Hence, the minimum radius is 2TρL

subject: Physics
q: Surface tension of a soap bubble is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math> ]
question: Surface tension of a soap bubble is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math> ]
solution:
As we know,

Work done = change in surface energy of soap bubble

Therefore,

Work done =2T4πr22-4πr12

=8πTr22-r12

=8×227×0.0272-3.52×10-4 J

=18.48×10-4J

subject: Physics
q: A capillary tube $(\mathrm{A})$ is dropped in water. Another identical tube $(\mathrm{B})$ is dipped in a soap water solution. Which of the following shows the relative nature of the liquid columns in the two tubes?
question: A capillary tube $(\mathrm{A})$ is dropped in water. Another identical tube $(\mathrm{B})$ is dipped in a soap water solution. Which of the following shows the relative nature of the liquid columns in the two tubes?
solution: Capillary rise $\mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{gr}}$. As soap solution has lower $\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{h}$ will be low.

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of upper layer of water in a river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Shearing stress between horizontal layers of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Depth of the river is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Co-efficient of viscosity of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
question: The velocity of upper layer of water in a river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Shearing stress between horizontal layers of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Depth of the river is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Co-efficient of viscosity of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
solution:
The viscous force formula is given by,

F=-ηAdvdy

Therefore, shear stress FA=-ηdvdy. Here negative sign tells us that the direction of force is opposite to the velocity gradient.

⇒FA=ηdvdy

Now v=36×518=10 m s-1 and if the depth is d

dvdy=10d

⇒10-3=10-2×10d⇒d=100 m

subject: Physics
q: Sixty four rain drops of radius 1 mm each falling down with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ coalesce to form a bigger drop. The terminal velocity of bigger drop is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: Sixty four rain drops of radius 1 mm each falling down with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ coalesce to form a bigger drop. The terminal velocity of bigger drop is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
At terminal velocity, gravitational force equals Stokes drag: $mg = 6\pi\eta rv$
For a sphere with mass $m = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho$, this gives $v = \frac{2r^2\rho g}{9\eta}$
Therefore, $v \propto r^2$
When 64 drops coalesce: $64 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r_1^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_2^3$, so $r_2 = 4r_1$
New terminal velocity: $\frac{v_2}{v_1} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2 = 16$
$v_2 = 16 \times 10 = 160$ cm/s
